$comment: The capability table, stated once. README.md renders it for humans; tests/test-table.sh
asserts the installed artifacts against it. Configs, manifests, prompt facts, and README
Vocabulary matches README.md's Entries section. `read`/`write` name a region; `egress`
names which destinations are reachable, not the route taken to reach them; `commands`
names how a shell command is treated.

command_outcomes.prompt_deny: The command prompts the user; if the user denies it, the command does not run.
command_outcomes.prompt_accept: The command prompts the user; if the user accepts it, the command runs as the user with the user's normal credentials and permissions.

secrets.why: denied to automatic tools, sandboxed commands, and `runbox`; deliberate shell access follows the `outside → prompt` command rule
secrets.$comment_kind: kind is data, not something an emitter can infer: a dotted last segment says nothing about it (~/.aws is a tree, ~/.config/git/credentials is a file). A tree needs a bare rule and a recursive one; a file needs one rule.

carriers.claude: permissions.deny Read() rules. sandbox.filesystem.* governs sandboxed Bash only; Read/Edit/Write go through the permission system and never touch the sandbox, so a denyRead entry stops `cat` and does nothing about Read. A Read() deny rule covers both, and deny is evaluated before ask and allow. sandbox.credentials is not a lever here: it affects sandboxed Bash commands only.
carriers.codex: filesystem deny keys protect automatic and sandboxed access. Home paths are denied in the top-level filesystem table; in-tree patterns are denied in the workspace-roots table so they do not become host-wide globs that catch OS trust-store files. Deliberate access uses the always-prompt run_as_user command carrier.

forbidden.why: readable, never writable without prompting, in any mode, because writing them changes what the agent is allowed to do

groups.why: a writable remote URL or hook redirects `fetch-all` or runs code; the rest of `.git` stays writable so `git commit` works
the escape surface and the box definition

excluded.~/.local/bin: the default helper and launcher directory is already outside every mode's writable region, while launchers dynamically protect the actual helper directory; a hard deny on the user's general bin directory would read as a guard the modes do not need

gaps.desired: in-tree secrets are unreadable inside the Research container for the whole session
gaps.actual: runbox's unreadable overlays are computed once at box_start, so a file matching a secret pattern created after launch is readable inside the box

gaps.desired: sandboxed commands read only the launched project
gaps.actual: the sandboxed leg denies the home tree and re-allows the project, because a command must read /bin/sh and the TLS trust store to run; literal workdir confinement is not implementable there

surfaces: MCP servers not named by the mode or a target carrier
integration_policy.why: a permission mode aims to control every user-configurable native route that can read, write, execute, or reach an external service; target-specific exceptions are recorded as gaps
integration_policy.caveat: Claude currently loads its default user, project, and local settings, and Codex loads its user config, so personal settings remain available; the user is responsible for ensuring that inherited settings contain no dangerous integrations
integration_policy.authority: Organization-managed policy remains authoritative on both targets and is outside the user-configurable mode matrix
integration_policy.trusted_inputs: Runtime artifact-root, authentication, and invocation environment variables are trusted launch inputs set before the agent starts; custom roots are not authenticated by the launchers

egress.why: wrapper guarantees, not OS ones: the no-argument helpers have exact un-injectable tokens, and gh-ro's own script is the gate

carriers.read: the presence of a bare `Read` allow rule. Built-in Read/Glob/Grep default to no-approval within the working directory plus additionalDirectories and require approval outside it, so a bare `Read` allow (meaning: never prompt) widens the region to `host`. `region: workdir` therefore means NO bare Read allow rule. There is no :workspace token and deny rules take no exceptions, so this is the only expressible mechanism; `allowRead` is not it, and the repo has none.
carriers.write: sandbox.filesystem.allowWrite plus permissions.additionalDirectories for the `plus` paths; sandbox.filesystem.denyWrite for `forbidden`; the session renderer also protects the actual settings, prompt, and helper roots when install-path overrides are used
carriers.egress: sandbox.network.allowedDomains for `any`; absent for `none`; sandbox.excludedCommands for `named`
carriers.mcp: an explicit per-mode --mcp-config plus --strict-mcp-config; an `mcp__<server>__*` allow rule exists for every configured server and no inherited server is loaded
carriers.commands: a bare `Bash` allow delivers `sandboxed auto` (without it, even a fully sandboxed loop prompts); autoAllowBashIfSandboxed gates the same; `ask: Bash(dangerouslyDisableSandbox:true)` forces a prompt on the generic route out of the sandbox (excludedCommands are the named, per-helper route out), because ask beats allow
carriers.web_tools: WebFetch/WebSearch allow rules, granted where egress.destinations == "any". Derived, not declared: the web tools are one route to unrestricted egress, so a mode that can reach any destination gets them and a mode with named or no egress does not. Codex renders the same fact as web_search = "live"/"disabled".
carriers.integrations: Claude's default user, project, and local setting sources are currently loaded; --strict-mcp-config still admits only the generated per-mode MCP file; --no-chrome disables the browser integration; launcher arguments and management subcommands that replace or mutate policy, integrations, project roots, helpers, or mode configuration are rejected

gaps.actual: Claude's default user, project, and local setting sources are loaded so personal skills and interface settings remain available
gaps.desired: inherited user-configurable integrations are disabled unless explicitly admitted by the mode
gaps.status: user, project, and local settings are trusted temporarily

carriers.egress: network.enabled, plus web_search for the web tools
carriers.mcp: highest-precedence generated overrides add the always-prompt run_as_user command carrier plus exactly the servers named by the mode, whose granted tools are approved; user-configured servers are inherited temporarily, while project and plugin-provided servers remain excluded
carriers.commands: Research uses approval_policy = "untrusted" plus a generated runbox allow rule, while the development family uses approval_policy = "on-request" so sandboxed commands run normally. Every boundary command uses the isolated run_as_user MCP tool, whose per-tool approval_mode is prompt; acceptance starts one child with the saved invocation environment, requested working directory, and invoking user's unrestricted host permissions. No-ask helpers still go through the mode-scoped launcher dispatcher.
carriers.integrations: each launch gets a temporary CODEX_HOME whose base config is copied from the incoming user CODEX_HOME, with the generated mode config and profile layered above it; the project is recorded untrusted so project .codex config, hooks, and rules are ignored; generated settings disable known hooks, plugins, apps, browser, computer use, native image generation, and shell additional-permission approvals, while user model-provider settings and MCP servers remain available; the actual config and helper roots are read-only; launcher arguments and subcommands that replace or manage policy, integrations, project roots, helpers, or mode configuration are rejected

gaps.desired: inherited Codex user integrations are disabled unless explicitly admitted by the mode
gaps.actual: Codex's user config is loaded so custom model providers and personal settings remain available; generated mode settings disable known integration features, but arbitrary user MCP servers remain loaded

gaps.desired: project configuration and reserved paths are read-only whether or not their parent directories exist at launch
gaps.actual: the rendered profile omits a nested read-only guard when its parent directory does not exist, because Codex 0.144.0's Bubblewrap backend otherwise fails before starting the sandbox; an absent guarded path can therefore be created during that session
gaps.status: nonexistent nested guards are omitted for Bubblewrap compatibility

notes.git_write: Codex's default sandbox makes .git read-only, so a workdir-writable mode re-grants .git and .git/** for local commits, then re-denies config and hooks. Verified: Codex resolves most-specific-wins even specific-vs-specific.
